body_txt: In many cases, creators will give their protagonist a unique hook that explains why we're following them and not, say, their next door neighbor. If the audience is expected to follow them and care about their adventure, we need to have a reason to be invested in the first place. Sometimes, this can be as simple as making them the odd-one-out in whatever group they happen to be in. The way this works is that the protagonist will not fit in with the rest of the cast for one reason or another. Whatever unifying trait everyone else has, be it money, power, talent, bloodlines, etc, this protagonist will lack. They could be a Muggle Born of Mages or a Penny Among Diamonds ; they could be a rookie thrust into a role they aren't prepared for , the only major member of their gender in the cast , or they could simply be from a different culture — maybe even a different time period . Whatever the case may be, they're different from everyone else. The story places emphasis on this, and as such, there are two primary reasons to go this route: conflict and intrigue. Conflict-wise, the differences will flare up and create some friction amongst the cast. The protagonist will likely struggle to fit in and find their footing, even if they have every reason to be where they are. Often, this can result in ostracization and bullying. If the rest of the cast doesn't see them as the outsider, there will still be ways to make it tense, such as if the protagonist lacks a vital skill everyone else has, or if they suffer from personal insecurities and think they need to prove themselves. Their nature may even bring trouble to others, making them almost a liability to have around before they shape up as a hero. Point is, the scenario is one that's full of story potential, no matter what the difference is or how the others react to it. As for intrigue, well, it's simply more interesting to follow the character who doesn't fit in. Seeing the story through the eyes of someone on the "outside" will allow for a new take on the subject, offer easy exposition , and give us a hook. In many cases, the thing that makes them different is also what makes them a good protagonist — not because it gives them the power to win (though it could), but because there's so many ways it can be used to expand their character. This is often used as a way to make the protagonist learn new skills, usually rare or personal ones, to get around their shortcomings. The story may focus on why they're different, how they got involved in the group, and what they do to overcome their baggage. In other cases, we'll get a look at a setting that isn't built to accommodate them, and watch as they navigate their life despite it. In short, following the outsider can give us a story we couldn't have had otherwise. However, keep in mind that the trope requires the differences to be a defining part of the story. It doesn't necessarily matter if the difference is a major issue — a relatively small difference can qualify in the right narrative. What really matters is that the difference makes them stand out in the cast, it makes their journey through the setting unique, and it influences their character arc to a degree. It cannot also start and end at "they're the hero / they're more powerful / etc.", as that type of thing falls under Unique Protagonist Asset ; While there's overlap, this trope requires them to be some sort of outcast or oddball, not simply "more special". For instance, an ordinary secret-identity superhero story wouldn't count, but if that ordinary superhero exists in, say, a setting where everyone else saves the world with wizard magic, it would . There has to be a contrast that extends beyond "the protagonist is powerful". This trope has overlap with: Compare Flashy Protagonists, Bland Extras and Hero with a Unique Name . A Ragtag Bunch of Misfits is what happens when everyone in the protagonist's group is a misfit to the wider setting, and an Alliance of the Alienated is when the oddball finds other outcasts to befriend. Contrast with Ridiculously Average Guy — unless, of course, being "normal" is what makes them different!
